network.overall_strategy: Integration of hydrothermal and surface prebiotic pathways: harness iron mineral-catalyzed CO2 fixation to key intermediates and their subsequent reductive amination in hydrothermal vents, coupled with surface Strecker and cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistries leveraging HCN and imine intermediates; this network provides redundant, convergent routes to L-alanine under early Earth conditions.

reactions.name: CO2 reduction to formate on greigite
reactions.mechanism: Electrocatalytic two-electron reduction of CO2 to formate on Fe3S4 surface
reactions.reasoning: Analogous to [Fe4S4]-mediated CO2 reduction; greigite catalyzes formate formation (Martin & Russell, 2007)

reactions.name: CO2 hydrogenation to pyruvate on magnetite
reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type C–C coupling and hydrogenation to yield pyruvate
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate synthesis via CO2 reduction on magnetite surfaces (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997)

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate to L-alanine on green rust
reactions.mechanism: Formation of imine intermediate and subsequent hydrogenation to amino acid
reactions.reasoning: Green rust catalyzes pyruvate amination to alanine at 70–100°C (Barge et al., 2019)

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of acetaldehyde
reactions.reasoning: Acetaldehyde produced via FT-type reactions on magnetite (Russell et al., 1990)

reactions.name: HCN formation from methane and ammonia
reactions.mechanism: Thermal dehydrogenation and coupling of CH4 and NH3 to HCN
reactions.reasoning: Volcanic spark synthesis of HCN in reducing environments (Miller, 1955)

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Imine formation followed by cyanide addition
reactions.reasoning: Classical Strecker synthesis of α-aminonitriles under prebiotic conditions (Adams, 1958)

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of α-aminonitrile to alanine
reactions.mechanism: Acid/base-catalyzed nitrile hydrolysis to amino acid
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of Strecker intermediates to racemic amino acids (Strecker, 1861)

reactions.name: Photoredox formation of formaldimine from HCN
reactions.mechanism: Cu-mediated photoredox dehydrogenation of cyanide to imine
reactions.reasoning: Cyanosulfidic photochemistry generates key imine intermediates (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012)

reactions.name: Cyanation of formaldimine to aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide to imine
reactions.reasoning: Formaldimine cyanation under surface conditions yields aminonitriles (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012)

pathways.name: Hydrothermal direct reductive amination
pathways.description: Pyruvate formed by CO2 hydrogenation is directly aminated to L-alanine in vents

pathways.name: Formate-mediated hydrothermal pathway
pathways.description: CO2 reduced to formate, then to pyruvate and finally aminated to alanine

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface Strecker
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde from vents undergoes surface Strecker and hydrolysis to yield alanine

pathways.name: Surface Strecker synthesis
pathways.description: Classical surface Strecker of acetaldehyde yields aminonitrile and its hydrolysis to alanine

pathways.name: Imine photoredox route
pathways.description: HCN and H2S photochemistry forms formaldimine, cyanation to aminonitrile, then hydrolysis to alanine

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN-driven Strecker
pathways.description: Vent‐produced HCN enters surface Strecker to give alanine

pathways.name: HCN to imine to Strecker
pathways.description: Hydrothermal HCN undergoes photoredox imine formation then Strecker and hydrolysis

pathways.name: HCN then acetaldehyde Strecker
pathways.description: Vent‐produced HCN and acetaldehyde converge in surface Strecker to alanine

pathways.name: Direct vent amination
pathways.description: Pyruvate and ammonia available from vents directly yield alanine

pathways.name: Direct nitrile hydrolysis
pathways.description: Preformed aminonitrile is hydrolyzed to yield alanine in a single step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Solid direct hydrothermal route; magnetite-mediated CO2-to-pyruvate step is less established.
suggestions: Provide experimental support or catalyst details for CO2 hydrogenation to pyruvate on magnetite.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Formate production is plausible but not integrated into pyruvate synthesis; network bypasses formate assimilation.
suggestions: Add or specify a formate-to-pyruvate conversion step to link formate into the pathway.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Well‐supported Fischer–Tropsch to Strecker route with clear environmental transition.
suggestions: Optionally specify pH and mineral catalysts for hydrolysis step to enhance realism.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Efficient two‐step Strecker and hydrolysis on surface; highly plausible under prebiotic conditions.
suggestions: Include clay or phosphate minerals to catalyze imine formation and hydrolysis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Elegant photoredox imine route with formaldimine key intermediate; minor catalyst details missing.
suggestions: Specify photoredox catalysts (e.g., Cu(I)/Cu(II)) and UV flux to ground plausibility.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN generation step is oversimplified; downstream Strecker and hydrolysis are solid.
suggestions: Correct or expand the HCN formation mechanism (e.g., include CH4 or formate pathways).

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Multi‐step HCN→imine→nitrile is coherent, but HCN origin is weak.
suggestions: Refine the HCN synthesis step with proper precursors and conditions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines hydrothermal acetaldehyde and surface Strecker well; HCN step needs correction.
suggestions: Update HCN formation mechanism or source to ensure chemical plausibility.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Direct amination is highly plausible, but pyruvate source is not included.
suggestions: Include pyruvate generation (e.g., rxn_002) as a prerequisite for this step.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrolysis is feasible but lacks an aminonitrile precursor in the pathway.
suggestions: Add a Strecker or cyanation step to supply α‐aminonitrile before hydrolysis.

evaluation.network_feedback: Overall, the network integrates hydrothermal and surface routes with robust hub connectivity. Key intermediates and convergence points are well defined. Improvements are needed for the magnetite‐catalyzed pyruvate step and the HCN generation mechanism in several pathways. Linking formate to pyruvate and ensuring aminonitrile supply for direct hydrolysis would enhance coherence.
